details.text: Derivative: حَدِيثُ refers to the news relating to something that manifests like an event or an occurrence. It is also used with the meaning of something that is new. It can be used for something that isn’t knew but has been forgotten or attention is not being given to it as if it is something new that needs to be paid heed to.

Derivative: الْغَاشِيَةِ comes from the verb غَشِيَ – يَغْشَى – غِشاوَة and it means to cover something up entirely. It is being used here to describe the event of the Day of Judgement which will overwhelm and engulf everything.

هَلْ أَتَاكَ حَدِيثُ الْغَاشِيَةِ

- “Has the news of the overwhelming event come to you?” – The use of this style of questioning has been said to be for the purpose of confirming the matter being questioned i.e., Hasn’t this news already reached you? Of course it has”. Another purpose of this form of questioning is to draw the interest and attention of the listener.

Derivative: لَاغِيَةً is from لَغَا – يَلْغُو – لَغْوُا and it means to talk nonsense and refers to all kinds of speech that has no benefit in it. It can include all kinds of crude, vulgar, thoughtless and shameless speech.
Derivative: سُرُرٌ is the plural of سَرِيرٌ which in modern Arabic refers to a bed. In classical Arabic سَرِيرٌ refers to a couch that is reclined upon which only the rich had.

Derivative: مَرْفُوعَةٌ means to be raised and elevated and can also include the implication of grandeur and being seated high. مَرْفُوعَةٌ also implies that they have been raised for you without any effort on your behalf.

Derivative: مَوْضُوعَةٌ is from the verb وَضَعَ – وَضْعٌ and means to be placed down and the use of the أِسْم المَفْعول (Ism ul-Maf’ool) form implies that it is done without any effort on your behalf and that they will constantly be placed down.
Derivative: أَكْوَابٌ is the plural of كُوبٌ and refers to a cup that doesn’t have handles like glasses which are wider on the top.
Derivative: نَمَارِقُ refers to small cushions which are reclined upon.
Derivative: زَرَابِيُّ refers to luxurious large rugs.

Derivative: نُصِبَتْ is from the words that can have opposite meanings and can mean to raise and erect, just as it can mean to plant something. The word here therefore encapsulates how mountains are firmly planted and raised high.

Derivative: خَاشِعَةٌ refers to a fear that makes your muscles turn weak and starts to paralyse you. The meaning here would be “overwhelmed with fear, so much so that their will be a numbness on them”. However, the majority of the Mufassireen interpret the meaning of خَشَعَ – خُشوع and خَاِشِع to mean humbled, submissive and diminished in a state of humiliation and lowliness. The faces being described as خَاشِعَةٌ can be physically described as being downcast i.e., in a humbled state that has lost hope.

Derivative: وُجُوهٌ (Faces) - The use of the indefinite (نَكِرَة - Nakirah) form with Tanween (ٌ) instead of the definite (مَعْرِفَة - Ma'rifah) form with Alif Laam (الْ) indicates that it is not talking about all the faces but rather some of them, even if that “some” is understood to refer to many faces.

وُجُوهٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ خَاشِعَةٌ

- “Faces (وُجُوهٌ) on that day (يَوْمَئِذٍ) will be Khaashi’ah (خَاشِعَةٌ)”. The standard Arabic sentence structure would place يَوْمَئِذٍ (on that day) at the end of the sentence, however it has been brought forward here for the purpose of الاِخْتِصَاص (Al-Ikhtisaas – exclusivity), so that the meaning becomes: “Faces, especially on that day, will be Khaashi’ah”.
Derivative: سُطِحَتْ from سَطَحَ and it carries the meaning of being spread out and also being levelled, smoothed and flattened out.
Derivative: مُصَيْطِرٍ is someone who has gained complete control and commanding authority over something or someone and compels others to follow.
Derivative: إِلَّا as well as meaning “except” can also mean لَكِنَّ “However”. And that is the meaning here.

Derivative: الْعَذَابَ comes from the word عَذَبَ which is used in Arabic when someone leaves eating due to severe thirst. عَذاب comes from this and refers to one of the worst kinds of torment that one can go through. It refers to a painful and torturous punishment. عَذَّبَ – تَعْذِيب means to inflict that painful punishment and torment “الْعَذَابَ”.
Derivative: إِيَابَ is from آَبَ – يَؤُوبُ which means to return and implies the final return or the return to the final intended destination.

- عَلى attached a pronoun like كَ كِ ي هُ هَا means that something has been mandated and obliged upon the possessor of that attached pronoun. عَلى + نَا means “It has been mandated upon us” or “We have taken it upon ourselves” i.e., to undertake a certain action or responsibility”.
Derivative: نَّاصِبَةٌ from نَصَبَ – نَصَبٌ which can hold the meaning to be exhausted and worn out.

Derivative: عَامِلَةٌ means working and here it is an expression of being made to work hard. It can refer to their faces on the Day of Judgement due to what they encounter. It has also been said to refer how the possessors of these faces were in the life of the world in terms of expending their efforts and working hard in that which would bring them no benefit in the hereafter. Both meanings can be combined to provide the image of someone who has toiled in the life of the world in that which will bring him no benefit in the hereafter and then on the Day of Judgment he will be faced by even greater toils and burdens.

Derivative: حَامِيَةً comes from the verb حَمِيَ which means to become hot. حَامِيَةً refers to intense or scorchingly hot. It has also been argued that it can come from the verb حَمَا which means to protect, in the case where the fire is such that it acts differently from normal fire in terms of its effects whereby the person remains continuously exposed to it whilst being kept or protected from perishing.
Derivative: تَصْلَىٰ from صَلَى has been interpreted to mean to enter and it also means to burn, or by combining the meanings, it means to be exposed to burning.
Derivative: آنِيَةٍ comes from أَنَى – يَأْنِي which means to reach the utmost or maximum limit or extent and refers here to the heat of the water i.e., when it has reached its maximum temperature.

Derivative: ضَرِيعٍ refers to a repulsive and bitter dried out plant, shrub or tree of thorns which the people of hellfire are provided to eat from. Parallels are drawn to a plant called Shibriq that the Arabs were accustomed to which was poisonous and had large sharp thorns and no animals would approach to eat from apart from a camel.
Derivative: يُغْنِي from أَغْنَى which means to be free of need or to suffice and be satisfied.
Derivative: يُسْمِنُ is from أَسْمَنَ which literally means to make fat or to fatten and can carry the meaning of to provide nutrition or nourishment.

Derivative: نَاعِمَةٌ comes from نُعُومَة (Nu’oomah) which means softness and tenderness which is derived from the verb نَعَمَ which means to have ease and comfort (in life). In respect to faces, نَاعِمَةٌ indicates the absence of any signs of stress, freshness, happiness and being full of joy. It is the face you have when you receive a gift, when you receive a نِعْمَة (blessing and favour).
Derivative: سَعْيِ is from the verb سَعَى and refers to the effort and endeavour that someone put into something i.e., what someone strove for and pursued in life.
